title: Those with urges tend to lose — earliest impulse-control aphorism
html:
TL;DR

  • Two-line March 2019 self-aphorism: "Those who can suppress the urges lose / Those who can't control their urges will lose."
  • The note is internally contradictory on its face — both suppression AND lack-of-control end in losing — which is the interesting part: 16-year-old Yahir is mid-sentence working out the urge problem without resolving it.
  • Earliest dated impulse-control aphorism in the corpus; predates the no runway discipline framing by years.
  • Sits in the grants pass teen era aphorism cluster alongside don't sell the farm and fear of failing.
  • Reads as proto-fear of not doing — both flavors of not-acting (suppression and impulse) lose.

Notes

The line should read "Those who can't suppress the urges lose" — read with the missing negation, the aphorism resolves into a clean stoic-flavored discipline note. Without the fix, the two lines eat each other: suppress and you lose; can't-control and you lose too. Either reading is interesting. The unfixed version is the more honest snapshot of the teen working out the urge tax in real time — both moves cost.

This is the earliest of Yahir's impulse-and-discipline aphorisms and rhymes forward into the "hide behind Bill Gates books because you're too sad to admit" (2019 10 12 bill gates books cope) and the "don't do it for the money, do it for the thing that changes lives" (2019 10 30 do it for the thing that changes lives) lines. The teen is building a private-aphorism vocabulary about what costs you long-term.

Privacy note

None.

Connections

  • 2019 03 14 mr phillips substitute — same-week aphorism cluster
  • 2019 05 20 if youre so afraid of failing youll never experience the win — fear-axis sibling
  • no runway discipline · fear of not doing
  • grants pass teen era
  • urge as tax — concept stub created from this note
